data.title: IYR: Cool Inflation Prints Bode Well For Real Estate Stocks
data.description:
Inflation was the big story this past week. More tame price data revealed that tariffs’ sting has not yet hit consumers’ pocketbooks. Both the CPI and PPI reports came in below estimates. In fact, it was the fourth consecutive weaker-than-forecast headline CPI print, while core PPI drifted down to just 3.0% year-on-year.

Stocks wavered, while Treasuries caught a bid in response to the May inflation updates. Typically, lower interest rates would be a boon to the Real Estate sector. Month to date through the 11th, the iShares U.S. Real Estate ETF (NYSEARCA:IYR) was up by less than 1%, though.

I see that as an opportunity, given the prospects for lower Treasury yields. I was bullish on IYR last December, but the fund has merely trodden water, performing in line with the S&P 500 over the past six months. I reiterate a buy rating today.

Back Out Shelter ex-Hotels, and There Was Deflation in May

U.S. Treasury Rates Steady Since Late 2022

According to the issuer, IYR seeks to track the investment results of an index composed of U.S. equities in the real estate sector. The fund offers investors exposure to U.S. real estate companies and REITs, which invest in real estate directly and trade like stocks, with targeted access to domestic real estate stocks and REITs. IYR can be used to diversify your portfolio, seek income, or express a sector view.

IYR has shrunk significantly since Q4 last year. Total assets under management is now just $3.8 billion, down $1.0 billion since my previous analysis. Its annual expense ratio is low to moderate, 39 basis points, while the trailing 12-month dividend yield is materially above that of the S&P 500 at 2.5% as of June 11, 2025.

IYR’s share-price momentum has been tepid for quite some time. Its price is close to unchanged from its 2007 all-time high, and IYR is off almost 20% from the early 2022 all-time high. The zenith was notched when interest rates were depressed, before inflation became front and center, and the Fed began lifting its short-term policy rate.

IYR is a risky fund, too, at least according to recent annualized standard deviation trends—certainly the wild price action from around and just after Liberation Day has elevated volatility readings. Still, the fund is highly liquid, given average daily volume of more than 5.4 million shares, and a median 30-day bid/ask spread of only a single basis point, per iShares.

For an update on the portfolio, IYR’s allocation plots across the style box, which suggests ample style and size diversification. 15% of the product is considered growth, while 19% is classified as value by Morningstar. The majority of its holdings are considered mid-cap.

Though the stated price-to-earnings ratio is very high, Goldman Sachs normalizes adjusted funds from operations (AFFO) into earnings per share to find that the forward non-GAAP P/E is closer to 17, which is near to slightly below historical averages, particularly relative to the S&P 500 earnings multiple.

IYR: Portfolio & Factor Profiles

S&P 500 Sector Valuations: Real Estate Among the Cheaper Sectors

IYR is a somewhat concentrated ETF—almost half of the portfolio is invested in its top 10 stocks, so paying attention to the top names’ fundamentals and technicals is important.

I’m disappointed to see that the sum of dividends has been sideways in recent years. It’s my hope that higher interest rates could lead to larger distributions to shareholders over the coming quarters.

IYR: Holdings & Dividend Information

With clear near-term headwinds, but market interest rates that could be easing soon, the seasonal view is conducive to gains.

June and July have historically been the strongest back-to-back months when scanning price returns over the past 10 years, per Seeking Alpha’s data.

IYR: Bullish June-July Trends

The Technical Take

With macro factors in flux in the property space, IYR’s technical situation is mixed. Notice in the chart below that shares are near at a downtrend resistance line that began right around the time of my previous assessment. The ETF printed a bearish double top pattern in 2024, too, so the bulls have asserted themselves in recent quarters.

Also, take a look at where the long-term 200-day moving average—it's flat in its slope, asserting that there’s an ongoing battle between the bulls and bears. Moreover, IYR’s price has initially backed away from the 200dma.

Additionally, the RSI momentum oscillator at the top of the graph remains in a bearish zone between 20 and 60—I'd like to see price rally above the downtrend line and 200dma, along with a bump in the RSI, to help confirm a bullish reversal.

IYR: Shares At Key Resistance, Monitoring Momentum Trends

The Bottom Line

I have a buy rating on IYR. I’m not a fan of the technicals right now, but the prospect of lower market interest rates should eventually bring investors back into the real estate space.

data.title: Adding To My Amcor PLC Position
data.description:
It’s been just under 13 months since I announced to a disinterested world that I was taking a small position in Amcor PLC (NYSE:AMCR), and in that time, the shares have returned a loss of about 5% against a gain of just under 14% for the S&P 500. Much has happened since then, most notably the company’s acquisition of Berry’s, and I thought it’d be a good idea to check in on the company to see if the bullish thesis remains intact. I’ll make that determination, as I frequently do, by looking at the financial history here, and by looking at the valuation, and comparing the stock to the risk-free alternative.

Welcome to the thesis statement of our programming, dear reader. This section is designed for people who want a bit more than they get from the bullet points above, but much less than they’d get from a full article. I get it. Not everyone wants to be exposed to too much Doyle mojo. So, I’ll be adding to my Amcor PLC position because I think the financial results remain impressive enough. More importantly, though, the shares are cheaply priced, and this remains one of the few stocks out there that offers a significant risk premium over Treasury Notes. Thus ends the thesis statement. If you read on from here, that’s on you.

Financial Snapshot

The first nine months of FY 2025 have been generally good in my view. Although revenue declined by about 1.75%, net income exploded higher by about 16.25%. This improvement was chiefly caused by a 2% reduction in cost of sales, and SG&A expenses, and a 13.4% drop in restructuring charges. These three reductions reduced costs by about $188 million from the year-ago period. I like the fact that these aren’t one-time events.

Turning to the capital structure, obviously, the increase in indebtedness is troublesome in my view. For those not keeping track at home, long-term debt has increased by about $1.58 billion, or 22.35%. On the bright side, cash has increased by approximately the same amount, up by about $1.588 billion. So, the effects on the balance sheet are a wash in my estimation.

Given the above, I’d be very happy to buy more shares if the valuation remains relatively attractive.

The Stock

If you subject yourself to my stuff regularly, you know that I like to write obvious truths in an attempt to sound deep. One of these is that the more you pay for $1 of future gains, the lower will be your subsequent returns. This is so obvious that, to my mind, it’s almost embarrassing to write it, but apparently many speculators haven’t received the memo. Anyway, this is why I try my best to buy shares when they are cheaply priced. The only way to buy shares cheaply is when other people are somewhat “freaked out”, as the young people say, about the business. In other words, you’re only ever going to receive a discount on an investment when there are sensible reasons to avoid it.

I measure the cheapness of a stock in a few ways, ranging from the simple to the more complex. On the simple side, I like to look at ratios of price to some measure of economic value, like earnings, free cash, book value, and the like. I like to see shares trading at a discount to both their own history and the overall market. At the moment, Amcor PLC is fairly cheaply priced in my estimation, with, for example, the PE at about 16 times, and a price to cash of only ~10 times.

On the more complex side, I want to move past ratios and try to understand what the market is currently "thinking" about the future of a given company. The more pessimistically priced the shares, the better. In other words, if the crowd is depressed about a given business, that’s a very good sign. If you read my articles regularly, you know that I rely on the work of Professor Stephen Penman and his book "Accounting for Value" for this. In this book, Penman walks investors through how they can apply the magic of high school algebra to isolate the “g” (growth) variable in a standard finance formula in order to work out what the market is "thinking" about a given company's future growth.

Applying this approach to Amcor PLC at the moment suggests the market is assuming that this company will now grow profits at a rate of about 1% from here, which is nicely pessimistic in my view. Given the above, I think the valuation is reasonable at current prices.

Amcor Stock vs. The Treasury Note

I like to quantify things where I can, and so I’m going to quantify the risk premium I expect investors to receive with this investment over the next decade. Specifically, I want to quantify, as much as possible, the answer to the question: "If we assume that Amcor’s dividend grows at the same rate over the next decade as it has over the previous 5 years, how much more cash would the shareholder receive over the 10-Year Treasury Note?"

I answer that question below for your enjoyment and edification. If the dividend continues to grow at the same rate over the next decade as it has over the past half-decade, the Amcor shareholders will receive about 39.5% more cash from the stock than they would from the Treasury Note. This means that Amcor remains one of the rare stocks out there that offers a positive risk premium. In other words, it’s one of the few stocks out there right now that offers a superior return to the risk-free rate. This is yet further support for my continued bullishness here, in my view.

Conclusion

To sum up, the financials are in fairly good shape, though I don’t adore the extra debt. The shares are reasonably priced, and at current prices, they offer a superior return to the risk-free rate. While I think the global economy is very near recession, and demand for Amcor’s packaging products may soften, the dividend remains secure. I think the gargantuan cash hoard also largely insulates this company from the vagaries of the business cycle. Will the next few years be choppy? Maybe. Will investors who buy at current levels generate superior risk-adjusted returns? Absolutely, in my view.

data.title: The Shapeshifting U.S. Government Bond Yield Curve
data.description:
The yield curve for U.S. government bonds is currently very unusual – it's U-shaped. As shown in the following, the curve has been shifting dramatically since COVID in 2019.

In addition to the changes in shape, also note the level of interest rates. Yields were very low – less than 2% – during Quantitative Easing (QE) in 2019 through 2022, and then they increased above 4% since 2023 when the Fed took its foot off the interest rate brake under Quantitative Tightening (QT).

Three Shapes

The yield curve for U.S. Treasuries is currently U-shaped, which is very rare, even though it last happened as recently as 2019 under the stress of COVID spending. The press has reported that the temporary deferment of most tariffs is due to the reaction of the bond market rather than the stock market. Interest on our national debt could cripple the economy and lead to serious inflation.

Normal yield curves are upward sloping because investors demand a risk premium for taking on the volatility in long-term bonds.

Sometimes the yield curve inverts to pay higher yield for short term, lower risk, T-bills. This unusual situation tends to precede recessions,

But the yield curve is very rarely both "normal" and "inverted" – U-shaped. One interpretation is that the impact of tariffs is expected to take a couple of years, so the prices of 2–4 year notes have been bid up, suppressing yields in that maturity range.

So here's my explanation for the current U-shape:

  • Long-term and short-term yields are normal, with long rates exceeding short.
  • Intermediate maturities in the 2–5-year range have low yields because fear in that time frame is high due to tariffs and global tensions.

Another Rarity: The Treasury Bought Back $10 Billion

On June 3, the Treasury bought back $10 Billion in short-to-intermediate bonds. I think it was to save money. The Treasury's previous largest buyback happened in 2000, at approximately $3 billion. The 6/3 buyback is huge.

My guess is that the Treasury issued notes in the 2–5-year maturity range at 4% interest to finance the buyback of bonds that paid 4.4% at the near-dated end of the curve. The 0.4% savings on interest is $40 Million on $10 Billion in redeemed bonds. You'd think that supply-demand considerations would have flattened out the yield curve following the buyback, but it didn't, so maybe my guess is wrong and/or $10 Billion just isn't enough to move the needle.

Conclusion

These are unusual times that bring to mind the Chinese curse "May you live in interesting times." The term structure of interest rates tells an important story that investors need to heed. Intermediate term bonds are being bid up in anticipation of the potentially harmful effects of tariffs. Moving to the left (shorter maturity) provides higher yield with lower interest rate risk. That's what the U.S. Treasury "arbitraged" on June 3 with $10 billion.

data.title: Pinterest: Put A Pin On New Share Buyback Machine
data.description:
The tariff escalations appear to have presented near term obstacles for the continued growth of the online advertising industry. Pinterest (NYSE:PINS) had been flying high heading into the chaos, but suddenly trades at discounted valuations due to the heightened uncertainty. The company posted solid results that did not show material impact from tariffs, highlighted by resilient top-line growth and margin expansion. The company maintains a net cash balance sheet and is repurchasing stock. While growth might moderate in the near term due to both tariffs and lapping tough comparables, the stock looks too cheap here. I reiterate my buy rating for the stock.

PINS Stock Price

I last covered PINS in February, where I rated the stock a buy on positive investor reception to strong fourth quarter results. That optimism has been washed out by uncertainty from potential tariff impacts, with the stock falling nearly 15%.

It is highly possible that the company eventually sees some impact on its growth engine over the near term, but its financial profile looks strong enough to give it time to weather any potential storm.

PINS Stock Key Metrics

The social media company has seen some struggles in recent quarters from a stock price perspective, as investors appeared to have lost interest after the company rolled out its third-party advertising partnerships. Even so, the show must go on. PINS saw monthly active users (‘MAUs’) grow strongly at 10% YoY, including 4% YoY growth in the crucial US and Canada market.

The company paired that solid MAU growth with 5% YoY ARPU growth. These results made PINS look a lot more like Meta Platforms (META) than the struggling Snapchat (SNAP), as the macro uncertainty appears to have negatively affected SNAP in isolation.

The company generated 16% YoY revenue growth to $855 million, surpassing the high end of guidance of between $837 million to $852 million.

Adjusted EBITDA margins jumped 300 bps to 20%, as the $172 million in Adjusted EBITDA surpassed guidance of between $155 million and $170 million.

PINS ended the shatter with $2.6 billion of cash versus no debt. The company spent $175 million on share repurchases, an aggressive pace which is a friendly reminder to shareholders that this company is highly profitable and management is committed to returning capital to its investors. Looking ahead, management guided the company to see up to $980 million in revenue, representing 15% YoY growth. While that represents a significant sequential deceleration in growth rates, it looked satisfactory, given the tariff uncertainty as well as the fact that the company is lapping tough comparables.

On the conference call, management called Pinterest a “shopping destination,” noting that 85% of their MAUs come directly to their mobile app, 10 points higher than in 2022. I found this data point to be rather peculiar, as at first glance these two metrics do not appear so closely related in the way management is implying. In other words, I’d expect social networks to have the vast majority of their users go directly to their app, and shopping platforms to see substantially lower percentages due to the dominance of Google Search (GOOGL). Perhaps management’s point was rather that compared to other shopping destinations, PINS sees more users go directly to its platform - this indeed would be a clear competitive advantage. Management was highly confident in the health of the business, noting that this was “a platform with declining users and modest revenue growth a few years ago” that has been transformed into a “secular share taker with a more resilient business than ever before.” Management noted that Gen Z is their “largest and fastest growing user cohort,” which is a surprising yet important data point.

Management noted some tariff impact, observing a “reduction in spend from Asia-based e-commerce retailers in the U.S. given the change in the de minimis exemption.” Management noted that they have seen some of that offset by “geographic diversification from some of those Asia-based retailers to our European and Rest of World user regions.”

Management noted that AI was helping to boost their operational efficiency, with a quarter of their code being generated through AI, up from 15% since the beginning of the year. The most important takeaway from the call remains management’s ongoing efforts to shift investor focus towards profitable growth, which may be important as top-line growth inevitably slows.

Is PINS Stock A Buy, Sell, or Hold?

At recent prices, PINS traded at just around 19x earnings.

In my view, consensus estimates look too aggressive as they expect minimal deceleration in growth rates moving forward, even as the company laps tough comparables.

It appears to me that perhaps investors are hoping that PINS’s smaller size relative to META might imply a longer growth runway, but I wouldn’t be surprised if growth rates decelerate to the 10% to 12% range over the next two years. With the company having already rolled out both Amazon (AMZN) and Google (GOOGL) third-party advertisements, I expect the company to eventually fade headwinds as it is unable to “keep the hits coming.”

Yet, at these prices, the stock still looks compelling. I expect net margins to hover around 35% to 40% over the long term. The current 5.7x sales multiple implies a 14x to 16x earnings multiple. That looks rather cheap in light of the net cash balance sheet, GAAP profitability, and share repurchase program. I can see the stock re-rating to at least 16x to 18x earnings (5.6x to 7.2x sales), or more if the company can sustain elevated growth rates.

PINS Stock Risks

It is possible that PINS is eventually disrupted by competition, especially META. While I might not be the right target demographic, it is admittedly unclear why Instagram will not take market share over the long term, even if PINS users are supposedly more mission-driven. PINS is highly exposed to market cycles, and top-line growth and profit margins might deteriorate in a downturn. It is possible that AMZN or GOOGL eventually end their third-party advertising partnership, though there has not been any indication of this being a material risk. The most pressing risk is that PINS is likely to be lapping tough comparables soon due to having already rolled out AMZN and GOOGL advertisements, as I previously discussed. I’d also like to highlight a fellow colleague’s report which suggests that users are unhappy with the increasing ad load, which might imply that these fears are already materializing.

PINS Stock Conclusion

PINS might see some pressure on growth rates in the next year as it feels the impact from tariffs and lapping tough comparables. Even so, it trades at a steep discount to META and trades cheaply in its own right. I like the net cash balance sheet and expanding profit margins. The company is repurchasing stock and management has shown a clear commitment to profitable growth. The strong financial metrics and undervaluation are what I look for in holdings of the Best of Breed Growth Stocks Portfolio. I reiterate my buy rating for the stock.

data.title: NXP: Decent Muni Yield With No Return Of Capital
data.description:
Tools should be used for their purpose

It is possible to put in a screw with a hammer, and it is possible — eventually — to get a nail in with a screwdriver. I've done both, such are my physical and technological skills.

It's also a great deal easier and produces better results to use the right tools for the right purposes.

Financial instruments are tools as well. Thus, we should pay attention to how they're constructed. This will then tell us what they're useful for—only if that concords with what we're also trying to do should we go on to use them.

Futures and options will certainly give us an exciting time, but a steady and risk-free income isn't going to be one of the outcomes. Municipal bond markets might, sometimes, provide small capital gains—but low-risk and consistent income is the game in play there.

Nuveen Select Tax-Free Income Portfolio (NYSE:NXP)

This is a closed-end municipal bond fund. Which needs a little explaining.

In a closed-end fund, we do not interact directly, as investors, with the fund. Rather, our buying or selling is with other stockholders—our buying does not add to funds under management. Importantly, this also means that our selling does not subtract from the fund. This means that a closed-end fund cannot be subject to a redemption cascade—where we all turn up and demand our money back at the same time.

This is important if the underlying is illiquid. For illiquid assets will have to be discounted to be sold quickly. This isn't much of a problem with bonds—tho' some smaller issues could be fairly illiquid—but I've seen at least two open-ended funds in commercial property liquidate under such pressures.

But, being free of that cascade risk also means that a closed-end fund can wander off-net asset value. This one doesn't, not very much at least:

A 5% premium isn't all that much, given how much other funds can vary.

This fund also doesn't take on leverage. Many — to most — closed-end funds do, about 40%. The cost of borrowing for the fund is lower than the coupons to be received, this juices returns to stockholders. But, as a risk as well—there's no guarantee the risk curve won't invert and so short-term money be more expensive than long. There is some effective leverage "The Fund's use of tender option bonds to more efficiently implement its investment strategy may create up to 10% effective leverage." but that's more a regulatory statement than anything else in, my opinion.

Bonds

Bonds are loans to people, they're just tradable loans to people. Standardized, if you like. They come in three major flavors.

Treasuries are lending to Uncle Sam. As he's not gone bust for over two centuries, we generally regard them as credit risk-free. This isn't — not wholly and exactly — true, as other governments have gone bust more recently. But on the other hand, if Uncle Sam does, then we're back to a shotgun shell economy and all have other things to worry about. So, Treasuries are regarded as "risk-free" as far as credit is concerned.

Treasuries are subject to interest rate risk. If interest rates change, then the value of bonds already issued will change. Yesterday, someone had to pay us 3% to induce us to lend to them. Today it's 4%. Those traceable loans paying 3% are worth less than those paying 4%.

This change in capital value is intimately connected to the length (technically, "tenor") of the bonds. The value difference is the net present value of that extra (or less, if rates are falling) dollar per year in interest. This is obviously more for a 30-year bond than it is for a 30-day one.

This then bumps into another issue. Which is that long-term interest rates are nearly always (see above about the inverted yield curve) higher than short-term. So, if we want to gain a higher coupon, and income, from lending to people/buying bonds, then we have to take on more interest rate risk by being in longer tenor bonds.

The next group is corporate bonds and companies can go bust. So, corporates pay higher coupons than Treasuries—there's credit and interest rate risk.

Municipals are lending to sub-national governments and associated entities. States, municipalities (obviously, the very name), and water boards, and so on etc. These can go bust. As it happens, they won't be very often, one recent estimate had default rates at 0.1%.

When we have credit risk, we want to be diversified. Want to spread our bets, not put all our eggs in one basket, etc. It is possible to buy corporate and muni bonds in $1,000 lots—but only at issuance and sell them only at redemption. In the secondary markets, the rules get more complex—European corporate bond markets have a minimum lot size of $200k par value to trade in extant bonds. That makes diversification expensive—too much so for us retail investors.

The answer is to be in a fund. The fund is large enough to diversify, we own a fraction of the fund. Yes, we've got to pay a fee for this, but that's usually worth the value of the diversification.

But munis carry both credit and interest rate risk, they should pay more than Treasuries (even if less than corporates). They don't, they pay less than Treasuries. So, why?

Income from munis is not subject to the Federal income tax. What drives investment decisions is the amount left in our wallets after tax—so, the tax break is valuable. But how valuable? Ah, that depends upon your income tax bracket, you the investor. This is complex.

Tax-equivalent yields

The way we work through this is to ask what yield would a taxable investment need to offer to equal the income from the tax-free one—the tax-equivalent yield? This still depends upon the investor's marginal tax rate (i.e., the rate on the next dollar of income) but fortunately, people work out tables of this for us:

There's no hard and fast rule here but a useful rule of thumb all the same. If you're a foreigner, or earning too little to be paying Federal income tax, then munis are not for you. If you're paying 24% or above as your marginal income tax rate, then you should seriously consider munis as at least part of your portfolio. Obviously, the low-risk, income-generating, end of that portfolio.

Finances

The usual things we want to know about a fund.

The universe of muni bonds is vast—249 positions are being selective. At 17 years, the tenor is middling too long—they're reaching for yield that is, but not excessively so. This also will mean changes in the capital value of the fund as interest rates change. This is so:

As interest rates crashed during lockdown, the ETF price rose. As interest rates rose back to normality, they fell again. Strike one for economic rationality, then—income-generating assets rise and fall in value as interest rates change.

The major positions are:

They have a significantly higher exposure to zero coupon bonds than many other such funds. That's just one of those choices we pay fund managers to take.

Then there's this:

Now, if we think through those numbers we'll end up with a bit of head-scratching. The payout is higher than the coupons being received, and yet, the earnings-to-distribution ratio is 99%. Eh?

The answer is that some of those zero-coupon bonds are maturing, and being repaid. So, finally, the fund gains the interest (or the capital gain which corresponds to the interest rate over the life of the bond) which is earnings to the fund.

This is the particular point I like about this fund

I've whined about several other funds just recently. NBB and NAC (both from Nuveen, same as here) and VMO (from Invesco). They're seemingly paying much higher distributions. Near double in fact at times. But up to half — or close — of those distributions are being funded by returns of capital. Which I tend to think is a bit of a swiz (archaic British slang for "not quite right").

What I think has happened is a realization that some invest simply on the basis of published distributions. So, boost — by the return of capital — the published distribution and gain more investors. But this then becomes an arms race as other funds do the same. Which is, as I say, what, I think, has been happening. It's not right nor is it wrong, it just is, and I don't think it's helpful.

Not helpful to us as investors, that is. For we might receive the distribution, think that's the income, and go spend it. If we wanted to withdraw capital, we'd just sell a few units after all. If what, we think, is pure income is, in fact, partially (even up to half) a return of capital, then we risk misleading ourselves over our own capital maintenance.

This, really, leads us back up to the top of this piece. The need to understand a tool before using it. We don't want to be those who just look at the published distribution—we want to know, properly.

This fund? "Distributions are currently estimated to include the following amounts from sources other than net investment income: 0% capital gains and 0% return of capital." It's not playing that game. Here, income is income and if we want capital back, then sell a few units. That's my preference.

Why I'm wrong

In the base construction and laying out of the benefits of munis and bonds, I'm not.

What the fund does is correct. Whether we want to be in the fund, well, that's more a matter of opinion. My opinion about distributions and returns of capital is just that—an opinion. A preference even. That could indeed be wrong, the usual proof of that being that it differs from your opinion.

My opinion

Municipal bond funds should appeal and do appeal to those paying higher rates of Federal income tax. That's what the tax breaks are there for, to encourage such investment.

If you're not a higher rate Federal income taxpayer, then forget the sector as a whole. You can gain better with less credit and interest rate risk in Treasuries. USFR, for example, has nearly none of either type of risk.

If you are a higher-rate income taxpayer and are looking for either portfolio diversification or an income, then munis could well be for you.

NXP is a perfectly sensible muni fund. Management fees are 0.19% which is a nice low number—particularly for an actively managed fund rather than an index.

I happen to like it simply because it conforms to my prejudices about confusing income with the return of capital. My prejudices are not an infallible guide to investing, do note.

data.title: PGF's Portfolio Review
data.description:
Co-authored by Strategic Yield.

Today's article is about the Invesco Financial Preferred ETF (NYSEARCA:PGF) portfolio. The fund invests primarily in the financial sector and traditional preferred stocks, with 90% of its holdings being fixed-rate. You can find more information about the fund in the following lines.

Fund Overview

PGF had total assets of approximately $782 million as of the end of the first quarter of 2025, distributed in 93 holdings. The current market capitalization is close to $1.22 billion. It has an effective duration of 9.31 and an average maturity of over 5 years. The total expense ratio is 0.54%, with 0.50% management fees. The 10-year total return on NAV is 2.90%, and the 5-year is 2.15%.

Below, you can observe a comparison of the 1-year total return between PGF and SP500:

85.97% of the fund is rated BBB, and 13.66% is rated BB. If we use the Moody's methodology,

And we find the assets, we divide them into groups, by type, as well as those that are traded below par and above par, and then we find weighted averages for each group by yield, credit rating, interest rate, and price, we get an average credit score of close to 10, which is a Baa3 rating. Let's show you exactly what we mean...

The holdings

Fixed-Rate Preferred Stocks > PAR:

Approximately 12% of the fund's portfolio comprises fixed-rate preferred stocks, which are currently trading above par. These instruments have a weighted average price of 25.86 and offer an average current yield of 6.17%. In terms of credit quality, their weighted average rating is Baa3, which places them at the lower end of investment grade.

Still not callable:

We present them by their yield to call and years to call:

Already Callable:

We present them by their current yield and stripped price so that one can estimate the eventual call risk by looking at the distance to par.

And the credit scores of the group:

Fixed-Rate Preferred Stocks < PAR

Fixed-rate preferred stocks, which trade below par, make up about 71% of the portfolio. These instruments have a weighted average yield to worst of 6.50% and a weighted average price of 19.86. Their average credit rating is Baa3. You can see details about them in the charts below, distributed by:

Yield to worst:

And credit rating:

Fixed to floating

Our approach to valuing floating-rate or floating-rate preferred stocks simplifies the calculation of expected returns by replacing these instruments with fixed-rate equivalents based on the current swap rate. We assume that interest rates remain constant after the redemption date, using 40-year maturity for the calculation, and calculate the expected internal rate of return on this basis. For ease of visualization in the charts, we also calculate the expected duration of such a hypothetical fixed-rate security.

Fixed-to-floating rate stocks based on LIBOR (SOFR + 0.26%) make up about 5% of the portfolio. Their weighted average current yield is 3.81%, their expected future interest rate is 3.87%, and their average price is 23.69. The yield to call amounts to 8.69%, and the average credit rating is Baa3.

Resettable Stocks

The resettable stocks represent 12% of the portfolio (with stocks that trade above PAR at around 7.5%, and below PAR at 4.5%).

Resettable Stocks > PAR

The resettable stocks, which are traded above par, have a weighted average current yield of 6.88% and an expected future interest rate of 7.40%. The weighted average price of these instruments is 25.53, and the average yield to call is 6.55%. Their credit quality is, with a weighted average rating of Ba1.

Resettable Stocks < PAR

The average weighted yield to worst of resettable stocks that trade below par is 3.59%, and the average weighted future rate is 7.73%. Their average weighted price is 24.10, and the average-weighted yield to call: 8.49%. The credit rating is a weighted average of Ba2.

Finally, we present a chart that summarizes all PGF floating rates and resettable stocks, as well as the calculated average XIRR values ​​for these instruments:

Conclusion

So, now that we know more about the PGF's portfolio, we can conclude that:

When you buy PGF, you are actually acquiring the following combination of assets:

  • 12% fixed-rate preferred stocks that trade above par. They have an average credit rating of Baa3, a current yield of 6.85%, a yield to call of 6.17%, an average price of $25.84, and an average remaining time to call date of 1.6 years.
  • 71% fixed-rate preferred stocks trading below par, with an average credit rating of Baa3, a yield to worst of 6.50%, and an average price of $19.86.
  • 5% fixed to floating stocks, with a credit rating of Baa3, current yield of 3.81%, future rate of 3.87%, price of $23.69, yield to call of 8.69%, and call date of 06/28/2028.
  • 4.5% resettable stocks < PAR with a credit score of Ba2, yield to worst 3.59%, future rate 7.73%, and price of $24.10.
  • 7.5% resettable stocks >PAR with a credit score of Baa3, current yield of 6.88%, future rate 7.40%, and price of $25.53.

data.title: Sprouts Farmers Market: Strong Growth And Future Expansion Makes This A Stock To Watch
data.description:
Thesis: Sprouts Farmers Market is close to a "buy" owing to its affluent customer base and expansion plans, but economic headwinds and a high valuation keep it as a hold for now.

Quality has become a major focus for many consumers, especially when it comes to food. Yet years of inflation, slowing hiring, and the prospects of damage from the ongoing trade wars have hit consumer confidence and their wallets, too. We’re starting to see belt-tightening, and I’ve already noted that value-focused companies, like Chili’s, are best positioned in this environment.

Sprouts Farmers Market (NYSE:SFM) is a more upscale grocery store, and you’ll likely spend more there than at Kroger or Walmart for a similar shopping cart of foodstuffs. By and large, I’ve been wary of more premium/expensive brands these past few months, but Sprouts strikes me as sort of an exception to those sentiments. Still, the high P/E ratio in combination with general economic concerns leads me to label SFM a hold, for now.

Softening Economy Has Largely Impacted Subprime Borrowers and Lower Income Households

I’ve already covered the general signs of economic deterioration and a shift towards value among consumers in several articles. To be clear, while I have found signs that the economy is softening, nothing is conclusive, and there is also evidence of economic resilience. I’m mostly going to skip rehashing my earlier points to instead focus on the divide between prime and subprime borrowers, and working/lower middle-class families versus upper-middle and upper-class.

A few quick points about the softening economy and shift to value:

  • Chili’s, which is owned by Brinker International (NYSE:EAT), has seen strong sales growth based on its value-centric promotions. Meanwhile, Chipotle has seen sales stall and decline, with many customers complaining about prices and portion sizes.
  • Credit card debt continues to rise, suggesting that people are turning to cards to maintain their lifestyle.
  • Rewards programs are becoming quite popular. Taco Bell has enjoyed strong growth in recent months, with its loyalty program, which allows consumers to earn free food, being one of the key factors.
  • During recessions, consumers generally reduce eating out and prepare more food at home. This is something to keep in mind going forward.

Based on the above, it’d be easy to label Sprouts a “sell,” but there is a key distinction that needs to be considered: Most of the deterioration has been in subprime and lower-income households. SFM, meanwhile, generally targets more affluent households, and their stores are often found in wealthier zip codes.

Auto loan delinquencies are on the rise, for example, but the uptick in prime borrower delinquencies has been relatively minor. The rise among subprime borrowers, unfortunately, has been rather steep, as demonstrated above. Credit card delinquencies have also been on the rise. However, once again, much of this is because of subprime borrowers falling behind, as illustrated below (while there is a small observed downtick in 2025, rates are still historically elevated).

Sprouts Targets Wealthier Customers, Who So Far Remain Resilient

Sprouts Farmers Markets tend to be located in areas with higher-than-median household incomes (HHI). This likely has helped shield SFM from inflation, as wealthier customers are able to more easily absorb rising costs. This helps explain why Sprouts continues to enjoy rising revenues and same-store sales (more on this later). It’s also a major reason why I’m not as worried about Sprouts’ performance, even as some economic indicators indicate a shift towards value.

Wealthier households are in a better position to weather short-term economic turbulence, especially if asset prices, including stocks, continue to make gains. Wealthier households are more likely to own their home, so rising house prices may benefit them. Meanwhile, lower-income households are more likely to rent. Rising property prices can produce higher rents, and the renter is not going to benefit from the asset price increase.

This doesn’t mean that Sprouts is invulnerable. If the economy suffers a severe and prolonged contraction, markets will likely sink as well. Companies may not be looking to fire/not hire white-collar workers right now, but a deteriorating economy could lead to more wealthy and upper-middle-class households taking a hit. Yet, at least for now, wealthier households continue to generally do well.

Sprouts’ High Valuation Has Me “Hold” For The Time Being

Based on the above, I would rate Sprouts as a buy. For now, however, SFM remains a hold, albeit one that’s very close to crossing the threshold into buy territory. The biggest hang-up for me is Sprouts’ relatively high valuation. Right now, Sprouts’ Trailing GAAP Price to Earnings Ratio weighs in at 36.55, which is rather high for a grocer.

Kroger’s (NYSE:KR) P/E ratio is a mere 17.83. Target (NYSE:TGT) scores an 11.07 (but isn't as focused on groceries). Walmart (NYSE:WMT) has a similarly high 41.59, but many analysts on Seeking Alpha have concluded that WMT is overvalued at the moment. I’ve reached a similar conclusion, although I do believe that Sprouts could be a potentially strong growth company (more on that later).

Walmart SA Rankings

Turning to GuruFocus’s DCF calculator to conduct a reverse DCF calculation, and setting the discount rate at 10% and the terminal growth rate at 4% (in line with typically observed global GDP growth rates), I find that Sprouts has to achieve FCF growth of ~20.15% to justify its current share prices. Using the same calculator (with the same assumptions) but switching to an EPS calculation, I find that Sprouts will need to secure EPS growth of a similar 19.8%. These numbers are quite high and will be difficult to achieve. However, in the next section, I’ll explain why I believe Sprouts could come in over the bar.

Seeking Alpha’s quant tools also label SFM a hold for now. At 3.43, SFM is in the middle of the hold pack, with valuation being the primary concern. This is a major concern for me as well, but if I were using the five-point scale, I’d probably rate SFM closer to a 3.8.

As for fundamentals, YOY revenue growth weighs in at 15.51%, which is quite high for a grocery store and suggests that Sprouts is in a growth stage. Revenues now top $8 billion. The company’s gross profit margin is a healthy 38.72%, while the net income margin weighs in at 5.53%, which is relatively high compared to the sector median of 3.95%. The levered FCF margin (TTM) is a healthy 6.55%, slightly above the sector median of 6.16%. The market cap weighs in at $15.84 billion. Same-store sales, meanwhile, are up a very encouraging 11.7%.

As outlined below, Sprouts is targeting strong expansion, and the balance sheet should support that. The company has $3,738 billion in net assets and $286 million in cash and short-term investments, according to Seeking Alpha data. The company will likely need to raise funds to support expansion, but this shouldn’t be too difficult given that SFM has only $2.450 billion in liabilities.

For comparison, Kroger has a $43.56 billion market cap, $52.6 billion in assets, and $44.3 billion in liabilities. (Side note: I consider Kroger a buy at the moment.)

Sprouts Has an Aggressive But Moderate Growth Plan Ahead

Sprouts is still a relatively small chain with just 457 stores. These stores are largely concentrated in the West and South, although they are starting to pop up in the Northeast. The company has announced plans to continue to expand in the Northeast and to also expand into the Midwest, where currently there aren't many stores. At least 35 stores are planned to be added this year, with more in the pipeline.

Sprouts aims to grow to at least 1,000 stores, meaning they’ll more than double. This high rate of growth makes the relatively high P/E ratio far easier to stomach, and it’s easy to see why investors are valuing the company as a growth company.

In my opinion, 1,000 stores is a big but reasonable goal. It’s somewhat common for chains to grow too fast and too densely, which could lead to underperforming stores and the possibility of individual stores cannibalizing each other’s customer base. Take McDonald’s, for example. Years of expansion led to McDonald’s peaking at 14,339 stores in 2014. After that peak, McDonald’s actually started to shrink its U.S. footprint, although it has since started to expand again.

I don’t think overly dense expansions are a major worry with Sprouts. The map above shows a relatively prudent footprint, with locations centered around generally affluent cities, such as Los Angeles, Phoenix, and Atlanta. Curiously, Sprouts has avoided Mississippi, which is sometimes considered the poorest state by various metrics. While I suspect Sprouts will eventually enter Mississippi, it’ll likely be a careful, targeted expansion.

Sprouts should do particularly well on the affluent East Coast, and likely will find plenty of success in the Midwest.

Why I Still Rate Sprouts Farmers Market a Hold

There’s a lot to like about Sprouts. I think they’re in a good position even amid economic turbulence. Their customer base should prove more durable and able to cope with inflation, at least for now. So why is SFM a hold? It’s a one-two punch for me: the high valuation in combination with overall economic uncertainty has me hesitant. On their own, I could accept the high valuation as SFM is a growth stock in my opinion, and as noted, while I do have concerns about the economy, SFM is in a solid position amid those economic circumstances.

If economic data continues to show resilience, I will likely raise SFM to a “buy” designation. If the P/E ratio were to drop to somewhere around 25, I’d likely upgrade the rating to “buy” as well. SFM is already pretty close to buy territory for me. Investors who aren’t as cynical about the economy should take a closer look at SFM. Likewise, if you already hold SFM, it seems reasonable to continue to do so.

data.title: Richemont: Calmly Awaiting An Inflection Point
data.description:
Two years have passed since rumors about a potential takeover by LVMH Moët Hennessy - Louis Vuitton, Société Européenne (OTCPK:LVMHF) surfaced, and Compagnie Financière Richemont SA (OTCPK:CFRHF) has answered the challenge.

The owner of brands like Cartier and Buccellati has been one of the strongest performers in the industry, sustaining positive growth momentum while many of its peers are seeing notable declines.

What is driving Richemont's outperformance? And can it lead to further gains? Let's find out.

Introduction To The Special-Occasion Thesis

Richemont is a unique player in the luxury industry, with a concentrated portfolio of jewelry and watch brands. Unlike LVMH, Kering (OTCPK:PPRUF), Hermès (OTCPK:HESAF), and others, Richemont barely has any exposure to leather goods or outerwear.

Across Cartier, Montblanc, Buccellati, and many more brands, Richemont is selling luxury items that I define as special-occasion goods.

For instance, I find a Cartier ring or watch as something that a reasonably wealthy individual will give their spouse for an anniversary, birthday, and the like. Whereas, an LVMH monogram handbag is more appealing to a rich customer looking to expand their collection.

The way I see it, this means that, generally speaking, Richemont should be more resilient as it isn't a pure "splurge." In addition, its products are more timeless in nature, which means the company doesn't have to be at the front of every trend, or constantly searching to introduce new and exciting collections all the time.

Of course, being a lower-volatility company is a two-way street, which means there could be periods when peers are growing much faster.

Such period was at the center of Richemont's underperformance until 2023:

On the other side, Richemont has been a notable haven through the ongoing industry downturn since:

All in all, I believe that Richemont's attributes should be a long-term competitive advantage, which is why I've been more positive on the stock than most of its peers.

However, I thought that it too hit a wall right around mid-2024, as valuation and growth prospects became unattractive, mandating a "Hold" rating.

It's time to revisit that thought.

Richemont Is Rising Above The Industry's Growth Challenges

Richemont's fiscal year ends in March, which means the recently announced quarter was also the end of its fiscal 2025. Recognizing that, we can get deeper insight into results for the beginning of 2025, compared to competitors who have only reported sales in Q1.

In the March quarter, Richemont revenues were €5.2 billion, up 7% on constant rates, driven by broad-based growth across geographies, with the company's increased focus on its retail channel bearing fruit.

Europe was up 13%, Americas rose 16%, Japan was up 22%, and Middle East & Africa increased 14%. The only declining region was Asia Pacific, which decreased by 7%.

While sales are at all-time highs, margins remain somewhat depressed, primarily due to FX pressures, as well as higher input costs. Gross margins were 66.9% in FY 2025, which is in line with pre-pandemic levels (when prices were far lower), and operating margins were 20.9% due to several one-offs and inventory realignment.

That said, operating profit grew year-over-year in the second half of fiscal 2025 (i.e. September 2024 to March 2025), reflecting positive momentum going forward.

Overall, Richemont's results are quite impressive and do justify a premium in my view, especially in light of the weaker results from peers.

Richemont Surpasses LVMH In The Luxury Leaderboard

Historically, Richemont was an underperformer compared to LVMH, both in terms of growth and in stock performance. As of late, this trend has changed.

At the company level, Richemont outgrew LVMH for four consecutive quarters, and it even came close to surpassing Hermes in Q1.

Comparing to the watches and jewelry segments of peers, Richemont's dominance is apparent, outgrowing both LVMH and Hermes by a significant margin over the past four quarters.

Valuation & Prospects

So, we've established Richemont as a unique and more resilient player, I think. Looking ahead, I believe that this should support about mid-single-digit growth for Richemont, with stable or slightly improving margins.

At CHF 155 per Swiss-Listed share, and $185 per ADR, Richemont trades at 28 times trailing earnings, and 23 times forward earnings.

On a forward-looking basis, Richemont is roughly in line with LVMH, Moncler, and Prada, and above laggard Kering.

To me, this is a fair valuation for Richemont, considering its relatively safe mid-single-digit growth trajectory on one side, and the lack of meaningful acceleration potential on the other.

Conclusion

Richemont is the dominant player in special occasion luxury. Unlike its jewelry and watches, Richemont's business isn't as exciting, as its defensive attributes put a high floor, but also a limited ceiling.

To me, those attributes justify a premium over the industry, which Richemont is getting at current levels.

As such, I'm still at Hold, awaiting growth acceleration opportunities, or better valuation levels.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: BioNTech's Acquisition Of CureVac Brings Complementary Method To Target Cancer
data.description:
BioNTech SE (NASDAQ:BNTX) and CureVac N.V. (NASDAQ:CVAC) entered into a purchase agreement whereby BioNTech itself will acquire all shares of CureVac to bring about complementary development in terms of two key areas, which are messenger RNA (mRNA) vaccines and mRNA-based cancer immunotherapies. This collaboration makes sense because of the complementary nature of both companies using mRNA in order to target patients with a wide variety of solid tumor types. The basis of this acquisition is set to bring about the ability to leverage each type of mRNA technology in order to develop candidates that are capable of changing the scope of what is currently offered as standard of care (SOC) treatment for patients with cancer.

Besides the use of mRNA technologies by both companies to develop immunotherapies against cancer, they also make use of lipid nanoparticles (LNPs) as delivery vehicles. BioNTech is already making good progress in terms of seeing the antitumor activity of its mRNA immunotherapy candidate BNT116 alone or in combination with LIBTAYO (cemiplimab) in targeting patients with advanced or metastatic non-small cell lung cancer (mNSCLC).

This is based on results released from its phase 1 LuCa-MERIT-1 study. It is now testing the use of BNT-116 in combination with LIBTAYO for the treatment of 1st-line NSCLC patients with PD-L1 ≥50%, in the ongoing phase 2 EMPOWERVAX Lung 1 study. The company it acquired, CureVac is in the midst of evaluating its mRNA immunotherapy candidate CVGBM in Part B of its phase 1 trial to target adults with newly diagnosed MGMT-unmethylated Glioblastoma (GBM). The goal was to have a "go or no-go" decision for this particular candidate toward phase 2 testing in the 2nd half of 2025.

BioNTech Acquisition Of CureVac Brings About Complementary Technologies

As I noted above, both companies agreed that BioNTech would acquire all shares of CureVac. The way the deal was set up was that each share of CureVac would be exchanged for approximately $5.46 in BioNTech ADSs, giving an aggregate equity value of CureVac at approximately $1.25 billion. The bottom-line is that when all is said and done, CureVac shareholders will end up owning somewhere between 4% to 6% of BioNTech.

The purpose of the deal itself was for BioNTech to expand its presence in developing immunotherapies. It already has a start in using mRNA to target tumors with BNT-116, and now with this combination, it attains additional mRNA assets.

Furthermore, it adds to its already extensive development of immunotherapies. For instance, the other area it is working on clinical development in terms of oncology would be the advancement of its PD-L1/VEGF-A bispecific antibody known as BNT327. It is using this as a backbone therapy to be combined with many other modalities to target patients with 1st-line triple-negative breast cancer (TNBC), small-cell lung cancer (SCLC), and non-small cell lung cancer (NSCLC).

All of these indications are currently being evaluated in three potential global registration studies. With BNT116 antitumor activity already shown, plus the ability to add CureVacs mRNA immunotherapy candidates into its pipeline, it further strengthens its mission to change the scope of SOC treatment for patients with cancer.

Remember above where I stated that CureVac was working on CVGBM as a vaccine candidate to target newly diagnosed MGMT-unmethylated Glioblastoma patients? Well, this particular mRNA vaccine immunotherapy candidate deploys off-the-shelf shared antigens. The same can be said about BNT-116, which I will be going over below, which uses fixed antigens (FixVac) that can be applied across populations or tumor types. In addition, both companies had products in their pipelines that could be used as personalized neo-antigen therapies to treat patients with cancers. In terms of BioNTech, it has a technology known as iNeST (individualized NeoantigenSpecific immunotherapy) which uses a personalized therapy approach. One key item to note about iNeST is that this technology is being advanced in collaboration with a subsidiary of Roche (OTCQX:RHHBY) Genentech.

Anti-Tumor Activity From phase 1 Study Using BNT-116 Is Gaining Stride Against Cancer

As I stated above, the company is currently in the process of using its mRNA immunotherapy candidate BNT-116 in combination with LIBTAYO for the treatment of patients with advanced NSCLC in the ongoing phase 2 EMPOWERVAX Lung 1 study. Excellent progress is being made with this trial and antitumor activity has already been established from the prior phase 1 LuCa-MERIT-1 study, which I will be going over below.

Before going over the clinical study data released from this phase 1 trial, I believe that it is first important to go over what this cancer is and what the possible market opportunity could end up being. In addition, I will be going over how the use of FixVac is being applied to target these patients with advanced or metastatic disease.

Lung cancer is a type of disorder characterized by the formation of malignant cells on the lung that form a solid tumor. Specifically, it occurs in the cells that line the air passageways and occurs when cells start to divide and grow in an uncontrollable fashion. A person is the main risk factor when it comes to someone getting lung cancer. This can also be followed by secondhand smoking and chemicals such as asbestos also being involved in the formation of malignant cells.

There are two types of lung cancer, and they are as follows:

  • Non-Small Cell Lung cancer (NSCLC)
  • Small-cell lung cancer (SCLC)

It is important to note that approximately between 80% to 85% of lung cancer cases are comprised of NSCLC and then around 10% to 15% of them are said to be of SCLC type. The global non-small lung carcinoma market size is expected to reach $23.67 billion by 2034. This is a very large market opportunity, and the hope is that the company is going to be able to use mRNA candidate BNT-116 in combination with other drugs like PD-1 inhibitors to boost response and/or survival rates.

In order to see whether or not the company is going to be able to effectively use BNT-116 in combination with LIBTAYO (PD-1 inhibitor) for the treatment of patients with 1st-line advanced NSCLC with tumors expressing PD-L1 ≥50%, it is in the process of evaluating this combination in the ongoing phase 2 EMPOWERVAX LUNG 1 trial. This specific study is set to recruit up to a total of 100 patients who are going to be randomized into one of two treatment arms of this mid-stage trial as follows:

  • Arm 1 - LIBTAYO
  • Arm 2 - BNT-116 in combination with LIBTAYO.

In essence, the goal here is to see whether or not BNT-116 in combination with LIBTAYO achieves a superior outcome in terms of the primary endpoint established for it which is the Objective Response Rate (ORR). A patient will be considered to be in response if they either achieve a confirmed complete response (CR) or a partial response (PR). It remains to be seen how the clinical data from this study turns out.

However, it is important to note that the company had already established positive antitumor activity when it ran the open-label, multiple cohort phase 1 LuCa-MERIT-1 clinical study. This particular trial is eventually to recruit up to 220 patients in total. However, in terms of the preliminary data obtained, it was done so from the evaluation of 20 patients. With a cutoff date of December 2024, this group of patients completed the combination treatment regimen of BNT-116 + LIBTAYO to treat their disease. It was noted that the best overall response was that of 9 out of 20 patients (45%) who achieved a partial response (PR). Plus, it was also revealed that about 7 out of 20 patients (35%) had achieved stable disease (SD).

All things being considered, this was actually solid data. Why is that? Well, it is because all of this antitumor activity data was accomplished to be in line with LIBTAYO in terms of Treatment-related adverse events (TRAEs) that were ≥ grade 3. Such events were observed in 3 patients in the BNT-116 group (15%) and then in 2 patients (10%) who took LIBTAYO.

These are very frail and sick patients, and typically 1st-line platinum-based chemotherapy (PBC) helps to extend survival in these non-small cell lung cancer (NSCLC) patients. However, certain patients are either too old to go through intensive PBC or have several co-morbidities that preclude them from receiving it. With that being said, PD-1 inhibitor LIBTAYO + BNT-116 (mRNA cancer immunotherapy) could be offered as a differentiated treatment option.

One technology that BioNTech deploys is known as FixVac (Fixed Vaccine) and the purpose of it is to deploy the use of the RNA-lipoplex delivery system. It is able to use a fixed set of tumor antigens (non-mutated) that are presented to antigen-presenting dendritic cells (DCs). This lines up the boot camp of the immune system to boost the innate and adaptive immune response and correlate with one of the tumor antigens to kill the cancer itself. With that being said, the 6 non-mutated antigens presented to DCs are: MAGE-A3, CLDN6, KK-LC-1, PRAME, MAGE-A4, and MAGE-C1. This creates an off-the-shelf therapy with a fixed number of shared antigens for a particular indication.

Financials

According to the 6-K SEC Filing, BioNTech had $18.41 billion in cash, cash equivalents, and security investments as of March 31st 2025. A good thing about this company is that while it is in the process of developing its mRNA immunotherapy oncology candidates, it is also already generating revenue with its Covid vaccine. That is, with seasonal demand for its Covid-19 vaccines, it was able to generate approximately $211 million in revenues because of it.

The downside is that the company doesn't expect its full-year revenue figures to increase at all. As a matter of fact, it expects this to be in line with what was achieved in the prior year 2024. It is guiding full-year 2025 revenue to be between $1,968.22–$2,547.11 million in revenue.

Risks To Business

There are several risks that investors should be aware of before investing in BioNTech. The first risk is the actual acquisition of CureVac that had just transpired. The reason why is that it is believed that BioNTech can use CureVac's mRNA technology to bolster its mRNA cancer immunotherapy development focus. There is no assurance that the technology acquired is going to yield positive data in upcoming studies, nor that any candidates from CureVac are ultimately going to be approved. The hope is that the CVGBM mRNA immunotherapy candidate is going to achieve positive data in the 2nd half of 2025 from Part B of the phase 1 study targeting patients with newly diagnosed MGMT-unmethylated Glioblastoma, but there is no guarantee this is going to happen.

A second risk to consider would be in terms of the development of BNT-116 and other mRNA cancer immunotherapy candidates in its pipeline. While there has been antitumor activity achieved thus far in several patients, this is only in terms of phase 1 early-stage testing. There is no assurance that, upon the release of data from the phase 2 EMPOWERVAX Lung 1 study, the primary endpoint of Objective Response Rate (ORR) is going to be met with statistical significance. Nor, that the company is going to receive a go-ahead from regulators to be able to advance BNT-116 and other mRNA immunotherapy candidates for the treatment of patients with other types of tumors.

The third and final risk to consider would be in terms of the development of the other immunotherapy that BioNTech is working on. This would be in terms of the development of its PD-1/VEGF-A bispecific antibody BNT-327 being developed for the treatment of patients with NSCLC, SCLC, and TNBC in three-phase 3 studies. There is no assurance that the final data from any of these ongoing studies is going to be positive, or result in the ability to be marketed under regulatory approvals. In addition, the hope is to combine BNT-327 with antibody-drug conjugates (ADCS). There is no assurance that such combinations are going to result in improved efficacy over existing monotherapies or BNT-327 alone.

Conclusion

BioNTech's acquisition of CureVac brings about a complementary development path for the company as a whole. As I have stated above, both are in the process of developing mRNA cancer immunotherapy candidates using different types of technologies. BioNTech itself has the FixVac and iNeST technologies to develop such candidates in its pipeline. CureVac has a different approach to mRNA translation to target disorders. The end result is one company using mRNA therapies to hopefully change the scope of the standard of care (SOC) in the oncology space. BNT-116 using the FixVac platform technology uses a fixed set of 6 non-mutated antigens found in NSCLC solid tumors.

It even has other FixedVac candidates in place, like BNT-111 which incorporates 4 non-mutated antigens for the treatment of patients with melanoma. Plus, another side of oncology candidates such as it throwing its hat into the ring is using PD-L1/VEGF-A bispecific antibody BNT-327 to target patients with TNBC, SCLC, and NSCLC. The acquisition of CureVac brings in mRNA cancer immunotherapy vaccine candidate CVGBM, which is being explored in a phase 1 study to treat adults with newly diagnosed MGMT-unmethylated Glioblastoma. With the acquisition of CureVac now established by BioNTech, plus the continued clinical advancements of BNT-116 and PD-L1/VEGF-A bispecific antibody BNT-327 to target patients with a variety of types of solid tumors, I believe that investors could benefit from any potential gains made here.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: SOXS: Losses Will Only Deepen From Here -- Bad Time To Bet Against Semis
data.description:
We don’t usually cover ETFs here, but there is one that we have to warn investors about: Direxion Daily Semiconductor Bear 3X Shares (NYSEARCA:SOXS). This is an inverse triple-leverage ETF of the daily performance of the semi-index, SOX, meaning if SOX drops 1%, SOXS gains 3%, and the opposite applies. So, this was the right stock to be looking at when U.S. President Donald Trump was heading full force into his reciprocal tariffs war; the YTD chart spike speaks for itself, highlighting that in early April, while everything was getting dragged down, SOXS was going the other way. SOXS hit $53.43 per share on April 7th before taking a massive nosedive on the 90-day pause back to the low $20s and has since slid off to $9.50 at Wednesday's close and trading around $9.70 in pre-market. The April pressure on semis is not only behind us, but the growth narrative is visibly back on and expanding.

Why is it the wrong time to be in SOXS?

The market already digested two “freak-out” moments within the semis; one surrounding the impact of tariffs and the other around AI. There remains dust in the air about both what will happen when the 90-day pause is up on July 9th and the AI bubble that's building back up. Having said that, we're looking at a cleaner growth runway heading into the second half of the year, so betting on trouble is equivalent to throwing darts in the dark. Just this morning, China confirmed that the trade deal struck with the U.S. is to be upheld and the world’s two largest economies have come to a trade war truce; this didn’t happen overnight; instead was the result of a lot of back and forth between Xi and Trump and additional talks in London last week. Commerce Secretary Howard Lutnick said earlier this month:

Everybody's talking to us. You're going to see, over the next couple of weeks, first-class deals for the American worker

Even if the trade war was back on, it looks like tech, and semis in specific, are getting a free pass; remember that back when tensions were still high in mid-April, Trump exempted smartphones, PCs, and other semi-chips from tariffs on China. So, it’s unlikely we’ll see another surge in SOXS similar to that in April on the back of this. This isn’t to say we won’t see volatility; that’s part of the game, but nothing that warrants holding this name for too long. SOXS is for short rides, not long road trips. If you hold it too long, the volatility will eat away your gains, which is what we call volatility decay, and we will address it in a minute.

The other factor is AI. Heading into April, semis with AI exposure were grossly overvalued, trading at premium multiples, and throughout April, we saw that the bubble deflated a bit. A big part of that reset was the U.S.'s new licensing requirement on Nvidia’s (NVDA) H20 and its equivalents like AMD's (AMD) MI308, which dragged down both stocks substantially. Since both companies reported earnings and even with AMD’s $800M write-off and NVDA’s guiding lower than expected for the July quarter, which essentially de-risked guidance, the stocks continue to push through. This goes to say that the trouble regarding U.S.-China trade wars, from current visibility, has been priced in. This week, NVDA Paris GTC is happening, and Thursday, i.e. today, AMD's AI Day is underway; NVDA is trading at $144.58 while AMD is at $120, up 17% and 11% over the past month. AI is also expanding in terms of geographic scale, especially after NVDA lost China; CEO Jensen Huang is pushing plans “to multiply Europe's AI computing capacity tenfold over the next two years by constructing 20 new 'AI factories' in partnership with local tech and telecom companies.” This will further boost semis and the AI story. The TMT conferences underway confirm more of the same, with even struggling guys like Onsemi (ON) noting at the Bank of America Conference signals of recovery in the industrial market, auto-bottoming out, and little concern about China. The semis aren't immune to a reset on the rally since April lows, and we saw that with Broadcom (AVGO) trading down post-earnings in spite of expanding custom ASIC business and eyeing new hyperscaler clients. However, we don't think we'll see a panic selling spree that would make SOXS attractive.

Don’t forget about the volatility decay

The other thing to keep in mind here is volatility decay, which is: “the tendency of leveraged ETFs to lose value over time in volatile markets, even if the underlying index they track ends up flat." This means even if the overall trajectory is upward, the losses aren’t fully recovered, and gains can be diminished. This makes for unattractive returns unless there is a bigger narrative underway, i.e., the trade war. Some prefer to refer to this as autocorrelation decay because it can be positive or negative but generally becomes more negative as leveraged ETFs need to rebalance holdings daily. This all feeds into the status of SOXS as high risk, high reward, but within the current moment, it leans heavily to the former. To simplify: if you have a paper cup with a tiny hole, every time you fill up your cup with water, i.e., to make money, some will leak out, and over time your returns won't be as attractive because gains will slip out.

Bottom line:

SOXS is a high-risk, high-reward, and we’re all for that when the larger narrative fits. The current backdrop is more favorable to SOXS' counterpart, SOXL (SOXL), and investors shouldn’t be tempted to think this will be reversed in the near term, especially as we head into the third earnings season of CY2025. Micron is set to be the first to report towards the end of June, and we're expecting the stock to trade up as the appetite for memory, specifically DRAM with HBM, expands. The chart below showcases the performance of SOXS and SOXL against each other over the past three months.

Semis have recovered substantially from those lows of early April, but that doesn’t necessarily mean we’re headed for an industry-wide reset because more new money is jumping into the semis on the AI growth narrative, and investors betting against that may find themselves highly disappointed.
